title: MathLesson Architecture: UML, Object Interactions, State, Rendering, and Authoring Workflow
description: A practical architecture lesson for understanding MathLesson v4.9.2, including UML-style diagrams, object interactions, functional/event-driven ideas, technical debt, and preparation for Vite + React migration.

sections.label: 1. Why architecture?
sections.title: Why This Architecture Lesson Matters
sections.content:
MathLesson Architecture: Why This Lesson Exists

This lesson explains the current MathLesson v4.9.2 application as an architecture, not merely as a file that happens to run in a browser. The purpose is to prepare you to maintain the current single-file version, create real lessons confidently, and later migrate the project to a modular Vite + React architecture without losing the core design.

Honest framing
MathLesson v4.9.2 is not a clean object-oriented codebase in the strict Java/C#/TypeScript-class sense. It is a single-file JavaScript application built around object-shaped lesson data, event-driven UI, functional rendering routines, localStorage persistence, and renderer adapters. The architecture is real, but its boundaries are currently implicit rather than enforced by modules.

The architectural thesis

The app is architecturally strongest where it treats a lesson as data:

  • Lesson JSON is the domain model.
  • Import/export are serialization boundaries.
  • Rendering is a transformation from lesson data to DOM.
  • Student export is a derived artifact produced from author data.
  • UX telemetry is an observability layer.
The core idea: lesson data is the center
flowchart LR
  A[Author] --> B[Lesson JSON]
  B --> C[Editor Workspace]
  B --> D[Runtime Renderer]
  B --> E[Student Export]
  D --> F[Interactive Lesson]
  E --> G[Standalone Student Lesson]
  F --> H[UX Stats]
  G --> H
What is the central domain object in the current MathLesson architecture?

sections.label: 2. System context
sections.title: System Context and Boundaries
sections.content:
System Context

A system-context view answers: who uses the system, what enters it, what leaves it, and what external services are assumed? MathLesson is intentionally local-first. It can be opened directly from the filesystem, stores authoring state in the browser, and exports JSON or student HTML files.

System context diagram
flowchart TB
  U[Author / Teacher] -->|imports JSON| M[MathLesson App]
  U -->|edits sections, quiz, code blocks| M
  L[Learner / Student] -->|uses exported lesson| S[Student Lesson HTML]
  M -->|exports lesson JSON| J[Lesson JSON File]
  M -->|exports student lesson| S
  M -->|stores drafts and settings| LS[(Browser localStorage)]
  M -->|loads CDN libraries| CDN[Renderer Libraries]
  CDN --> K[KaTeX]
  CDN --> R[Mermaid]
  CDN --> P[Plotly]
  CDN --> X[JSXGraph]
  CDN --> C[CodeMirror]

Primary boundaries

BoundaryWhat crosses itWhy it matters
Import boundaryJSON filesControls what becomes lesson state.
Render boundaryLesson sections, code blocks, quiz dataControls what appears in the lesson UI.
Persistence boundarylocalStorage keysControls what survives refresh.
Export boundaryJSON and student HTMLControls what can be shared or versioned.
Migration insight
In Vite + React, these boundaries should become explicit modules: importers, renderers, storage, exporters, components, and state.

sections.label: 3. Domain model
sections.title: Lesson JSON as the Domain Model
sections.content:
Lesson JSON as the Domain Model

The importable JSON lesson is the most important architectural object. It is the stable representation that survives outside the app. The app can change, but the JSON lesson schema should remain intelligible, versioned, and backward-compatible.

UML-style lesson data model
classDiagram
  class Lesson {
    string schemaVersion
    string lessonKey
    string lessonVersion
    string title
    string description
    Section[] sections
    QuizItem[] quiz
    Object plots
  }
  class Section {
    string id
    string label
    string title
    string content
  }
  class QuizItem {
    string type
    string stem
    string[] options
    number answer
    string feedback
  }
  class CodeBlock {
    string id
    string language
    string code
    string targetSectionId
  }
  Lesson "1" o-- "many" Section
  Lesson "1" o-- "many" QuizItem
  Lesson "1" o-- "many" CodeBlock

Core object responsibilities

  • Lesson: whole instructional unit.
  • Section: ordered content block, usually HTML plus embedded widgets.
  • QuizItem: assessment item independent of section content.
  • CodeBlock: reusable or inspectable code attached to lesson authoring.
Architecture analogy
$$\text{Lesson JSON}\xrightarrow{\text{render}}\text{Interactive DOM}\xrightarrow{\text{export}}\text{Student artifact}.$$
Which domain object stores the HTML body for one portion of a lesson?

sections.label: 4. Runtime state
sections.title: Runtime State and localStorage
sections.content:
Runtime State

The app does not only display lesson JSON. It also tracks runtime state: active lesson, saved imported lessons, editor draft, quiz answers, UX mode, notes, and telemetry events. Some of that state is ephemeral; some is persisted in localStorage.

State lifecycle for a lesson
stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> BuiltInLesson
  [*] --> ImportedLesson
  BuiltInLesson --> EditableCopy: open in editor
  ImportedLesson --> EditorDraft: select in editor
  EditableCopy --> EditorDraft
  EditorDraft --> SavedLesson: save
  SavedLesson --> ActiveLesson: preview / select
  ActiveLesson --> StudentExport: export student lesson
  ActiveLesson --> JsonExport: export JSON
  SavedLesson --> [*]

State categories

StateExamplePersistence
Domain statelesson title, sections, quizJSON and localStorage
UI stateselected section, active panelusually ephemeral
Student statequiz score, notes, completed questionsstudent localStorage
Telemetry stateclick counts, transition sequencelocalStorage until cleared
Technical debt
In the single-file app, state transitions are mostly enforced by conventions and functions, not by a formal state machine. During the Vite migration, state should be centralized and documented.

sections.label: 5. Rendering pipeline
sections.title: Rendering Pipeline and Renderer Adapters
sections.content:
Rendering Pipeline

The renderer takes lesson data and produces interactive browser content. The important architectural idea is that different content types should be routed to different renderer adapters.

Rendering pipeline
flowchart LR
  L[Lesson JSON] --> S[Section HTML]
  S --> D[DOM Container]
  D --> K[KaTeX pass]
  D --> M[Mermaid pass]
  D --> P[Plotly pass]
  D --> J[JSXGraph pass]
  D --> Q[Quiz / Exercise binding]
  D --> T[Telemetry binding]

Renderer adapter idea

A renderer adapter is a small layer that knows how to initialize one library safely. For example:

  • KaTeX adapter: renders math delimiters.
  • Mermaid adapter: renders diagrams and handles oversized diagrams.
  • Plotly adapter: creates plots and cleans up WebGL contexts.
  • JSXGraph adapter: initializes interactive boards.
  • MathLive adapter: initializes exercise math fields.
  • CodeMirror adapter: provides syntax-highlighted authoring.
Definition
A renderer adapter isolates third-party library details from the rest of the app. It should expose a small interface such as render(container, lesson), dispose(container), or refresh(container).
What architectural pattern best describes the layer that isolates Mermaid, Plotly, KaTeX, and JSXGraph details?

sections.label: 6. Import/edit/save
sections.title: Object Interactions: Import, Edit, Save
sections.content:
Import, Edit, Save: Object Interactions

The author workflow is event-driven. The user selects a file or presses a button; an event handler parses data, validates it lightly, updates state, refreshes UI selectors, and renders the active lesson.

Sequence: importing a lesson
sequenceDiagram
  actor Author
  participant FileInput
  participant Importer
  participant LessonStore
  participant UI
  participant Renderer
  Author->>FileInput: choose JSON file
  FileInput->>Importer: read text
  Importer->>Importer: parse and normalize lesson
  Importer->>LessonStore: add or skip duplicate
  LessonStore->>UI: refresh lesson selector
  UI->>Renderer: render selected lesson
  Renderer-->>Author: interactive lesson appears
Sequence: editing and saving a section
sequenceDiagram
  actor Author
  participant Editor
  participant CodeMirror
  participant LessonDraft
  participant LessonStore
  participant Renderer
  Author->>Editor: select lesson and section
  Editor->>CodeMirror: load section content
  Author->>CodeMirror: edit HTML / Mermaid / table
  CodeMirror->>LessonDraft: update draft content
  Author->>Editor: save
  Editor->>LessonStore: persist updated lesson
  LessonStore->>Renderer: refresh active lesson
  Renderer-->>Author: updated lesson preview

Architectural lesson

The app should make the selected lesson and selected section unmistakable because edits are destructive operations on the current draft. This is why the v4.9 line moved toward a full authoring workspace with more visible selection state.

Workflow risk
If the selected lesson or section is visually ambiguous, the user can edit the wrong object. In an authoring tool, this is not a cosmetic issue; it is a data-integrity risk.

sections.label: 7. Student export
sections.title: Student Export as a Derived Artifact
sections.content:
Student Export Pipeline

The student lesson is not the same thing as the authoring app. It is a derived artifact: a simpler standalone HTML lesson generated from lesson data and quiz data.

Student export pipeline
flowchart LR
  A[Author Lesson JSON] --> B[Export Builder]
  B --> C[Embed lesson data]
  B --> D[Embed renderer runtime]
  B --> E[Embed quiz / exercise logic]
  C --> F[Student HTML]
  D --> F
  E --> F
  F --> G[Student opens file]
  G --> H[Student localStorage state]

What should be included?

  • The lesson title and section content.
  • Quiz and exercise logic.
  • Renderer support for math, diagrams, and plots.
  • A clear-state mechanism scoped to that exported lesson.

What should not leak?

  • Author-only editor controls.
  • Unrelated imported lessons.
  • Confusing authoring telemetry modes unless deliberately enabled.
Architecturally, what is the exported student HTML relative to the author lesson?

sections.label: 8. OOP analysis
sections.title: Object-Oriented Analysis of the App
sections.content:
Object-Oriented Analysis

Even if the implementation is currently single-file JavaScript, we can still analyze it using object-oriented concepts: entities, value objects, services, adapters, controllers, and views.

Conceptual class diagram for a modular future
classDiagram
  class Lesson {
    +title
    +sections
    +quiz
  }
  class LessonRepository {
    +loadAll()
    +save(lesson)
    +remove(id)
  }
  class ImportService {
    +parseJson(fileText)
    +detectDuplicate(lesson)
  }
  class ExportService {
    +exportJson(lesson)
    +exportStudentHtml(lesson)
  }
  class RenderEngine {
    +renderLesson(lesson)
    +renderSection(section)
  }
  class RendererAdapter {
    +render(container)
    +dispose(container)
  }
  class EditorWorkspace {
    +selectLesson(id)
    +selectSection(id)
    +saveDraft()
  }
  LessonRepository --> Lesson
  ImportService --> Lesson
  ExportService --> Lesson
  RenderEngine --> RendererAdapter
  EditorWorkspace --> LessonRepository
  EditorWorkspace --> RenderEngine

Candidate objects for Vite + React

ConceptLikely modulePrimary responsibility
Lessondomain/Lesson.tsTyped lesson data model.
LessonRepositorystorage/LessonRepository.tslocalStorage and saved lesson access.
ImportServiceservices/importLesson.tsParse, normalize, validate, duplicate-detect.
ExportServiceservices/exportLesson.tsJSON and student HTML export.
RenderEnginerendering/RenderEngine.tsCoordinate renderer adapters.
EditorWorkspacecomponents/EditorWorkspace.tsxAuthor-facing editing UI.
Design rule
Do not migrate by copying the single file into React components line by line. First identify responsibilities; then cut module boundaries around those responsibilities.

sections.label: 9. Functional ideas
sections.title: Functional and Event-Driven Ideas
sections.content:
Functional and Event-Driven Architecture

The app is also functional in spirit. Many operations are transformations of data:

Functional view
$$\text{new state}=f(\text{old state},\text{user event}).$$

For example, importing a JSON file transforms the saved lesson collection; editing a section transforms the lesson draft; rendering transforms lesson data into DOM.

Event-driven state update pattern
flowchart LR
  E[User event] --> H[Event handler]
  H --> V[Validate / normalize]
  V --> S[Update state]
  S --> P[Persist if needed]
  S --> R[Render affected UI]
  R --> O[Observable result]

Pure functions versus side effects

OperationMostly pure?Side effects?
Normalize lesson JSONYesNo, if written carefully.
Render KaTeX into DOMNoMutates DOM.
Save lesson to localStorageNoWrites browser storage.
Build student export stringMostly yesNo until file download starts.
Record telemetry eventNoUpdates telemetry state/storage.
In functional-programming language, what do we call writing a lesson to localStorage?

sections.label: 10. Technical debt
sections.title: What Is Solid and What Is Technical Debt
sections.content:
Solid Design Decisions and Technical Debt

An honest architecture lesson must distinguish stable design choices from temporary implementation pressure.

Solid design decisions

  • JSON-first lessons: lessons can be imported, exported, versioned, and shared.
  • Renderer diversity: the app supports math, diagrams, plots, boards, code, and quizzes.
  • Local-first workflow: authors can work without a server.
  • Student export: deployable learning artifacts can be generated from author lessons.
  • UX telemetry: real usage can drive future design decisions.

Technical debt

DebtRiskMigration response
Single-file scaleHard to locate responsibilities.Split by domain, services, rendering, components.
Implicit global stateUnexpected interactions and persistence bugs.Central state store or explicit React state boundaries.
Mixed DOM/render/state logicFragile changes.Separate render adapters from UI components.
Renderer lifecycle complexityPlotly/WebGL or Mermaid refresh issues.Adapters with explicit mount/unmount lifecycle.
Large diagram ergonomicsUnreadable Mermaid or clipped preview.Dedicated diagram editor and bounded preview behavior.
Important distinction
Technical debt does not mean failure. It means the current design has succeeded enough to reveal where stronger abstractions are now necessary.

sections.label: 11. Vite + React map
sections.title: Migration Map to Vite + React
sections.content:
Migration Map to Vite + React

The Vite + React migration should preserve the domain model and improve the boundaries. The migration is not merely a packaging change. It is an architectural extraction.

Target modular structure
flowchart TB
  A[src] --> B[domain]
  A --> C[components]
  A --> D[services]
  A --> E[rendering]
  A --> F[storage]
  A --> G[telemetry]
  A --> H[styles]
  B --> B1[Lesson types]
  C --> C1[EditorWorkspace]
  C --> C2[LessonViewer]
  C --> C3[QuizPanel]
  D --> D1[ImportService]
  D --> D2[ExportService]
  E --> E1[KaTeXAdapter]
  E --> E2[MermaidAdapter]
  E --> E3[PlotlyAdapter]
  F --> F1[LocalStorageRepository]

Migration sequence

  1. Freeze v4.9.2 as a reference baseline.
  2. Extract lesson schema into TypeScript types.
  3. Extract pure services: import, normalize, duplicate detection, export JSON.
  4. Extract renderer adapters with lifecycle methods.
  5. Build React components around stable services.
  6. Maintain backward compatibility with existing lesson JSON files.
  7. Only then add new features.
Migration invariant
A lesson JSON file that imports into mathlesson.v4.9.2 should also import into the Vite + React version unless a deliberate schema migration is documented.
What migration property protects lessons already created in v4.9.2?

sections.label: 12. Exercises
sections.title: Architecture Exercises and Migration Practice
sections.content:
Exercises: Think Like the Maintainer

These exercises are designed to prepare you to maintain and migrate the project.

Exercise A: classify responsibilities

For each function or feature in the current app, classify it into one of these future modules:

  • domain
  • services
  • rendering
  • storage
  • components
  • telemetry
FeatureLikely moduleReason
Duplicate import detectionservicesIt is import workflow logic.
Saving lessons to localStoragestorageIt is persistence logic.
Rendering Mermaid diagramsrenderingIt initializes a renderer library.
Lesson Editor tab rowcomponentsIt is UI composition.
UX event logtelemetryIt observes usage.

Exercise B: draw a sequence diagram

Draw a Mermaid sequence diagram for exporting a student lesson. Include: Author, EditorWorkspace, ExportService, Lesson, FileDownload, StudentRuntime.

Exercise C: identify technical debt

Pick one future feature, such as a dedicated Mermaid editor. Identify which current technical debt makes it harder and which module would own the feature after migration.

In the proposed Vite + React architecture, which module should own Mermaid initialization and lifecycle cleanup?

sections.label: 13. Summary
sections.title: Summary: What You Should Now Understand
sections.content:
Summary

You should now be able to describe MathLesson v4.9.2 as an architecture:

  • The lesson JSON is the central domain model.
  • The current app is a single-file modular JavaScript application, not a pure OOP codebase.
  • The implementation combines object-shaped data, event-driven UI, functional transformations, renderer adapters, and localStorage persistence.
  • The strongest future architecture is a Vite + React modular extraction, not a blind rewrite.
  • The migration should protect existing lesson JSON files and preserve the import/export contract.
From current architecture to future architecture
flowchart LR
  A[v4.9.2 single-file baseline] --> B[Identify responsibilities]
  B --> C[Extract domain model]
  C --> D[Extract services]
  D --> E[Extract renderer adapters]
  E --> F[Build React components]
  F --> G[Preserve JSON compatibility]
  G --> H[Add new extensions]
Practical next step
Before migration, create several real lessons and record workflow blockers. Real authoring pressure will reveal the correct module boundaries better than abstract planning alone.

quiz.stem: What is the central domain model in MathLesson v4.9.2?
quiz.feedback: The importable/exportable lesson JSON object is the central domain model.

quiz.stem: Which boundary controls what becomes lesson state after selecting a file?
quiz.feedback: The import boundary parses and normalizes JSON files into app lesson state.

quiz.stem: What is a renderer adapter responsible for?
options: Isolating a third-party renderer such as Mermaid or Plotly
quiz.feedback: A renderer adapter isolates library-specific rendering details from the rest of the app.

quiz.stem: Which item is technical debt in the current single-file architecture?
options: Implicit global state and mixed responsibilities
quiz.feedback: Implicit state and mixed UI/state/rendering responsibilities are technical debt to resolve during migration.

quiz.stem: What should the Vite + React migration preserve?
options: Backward compatibility with existing lesson JSON where possible
quiz.feedback: Existing v4.9.2 lesson JSON files should remain importable unless a documented schema migration is made.

quiz.stem: The current app should be described as purely object-oriented.
quiz.feedback: False. It uses object-shaped data and OOP concepts, but the implementation is also event-driven, functional, and DOM-oriented.

quiz.stem: Student export is best understood as a derived artifact built from the author lesson.
quiz.feedback: True. Student HTML is generated from author lesson data and quiz/exercise logic.

quiz.stem: Renderer lifecycle matters because libraries like Plotly, Mermaid, and JSXGraph can require setup and cleanup.
quiz.feedback: True. Renderer adapters should manage mount, render, refresh, and cleanup behavior.

quiz.stem: In a modular future, localStorage access should mainly live in which layer?
quiz.feedback: Persistence should be isolated in a storage layer or repository.

quiz.stem: What is the safest migration strategy?
options: Rewrite everything immediately and discard old lessons
Extract responsibilities while preserving the JSON contract
quiz.feedback: The safe migration strategy is responsibility extraction with compatibility preserved.

quiz.stem: Which module should own Mermaid initialization in a Vite + React architecture?
quiz.feedback: Mermaid is a renderer library and belongs under rendering adapters.

quiz.stem: Real lesson authoring should inform the Vite migration before new abstractions are frozen.
quiz.feedback: True. Real lessons reveal workflow pressure and better module boundaries.
